narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your application, and I want to walk you through why our model saw a 48% chance of you not passing the bar exam, leading us to pass on your candidacy—it's not a judgment on your potential, but a story pieced together from patterns in your profile. At the heart of it was your Year 3 decile of 2.0, which stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward this risk; it suggests your grades placed you near the bottom of your class in those crucial later years, a pattern we've seen trip up many strong starters. Layered on top was your LSAT score of 25.0, the second-biggest factor, which falls on the lower side and often foreshadows challenges with the bar's intense analytical demands. Your Year 1 decile of 3.0 added to that picture, showing early promise that didn't fully hold, nudging the prediction further toward failure. Coming from a middle family income bracket played a role too, as it sometimes means fewer resources for the exhaustive prep that bar success demands. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.1, while solid, didn't quite counterbalance these, tilting things a bit more. As an Asian female studying full-time, those demographics and your commitment rounded out a profile where every piece aligned toward higher risk in our model's view. It's like the model saw you as someone with heart and hustle, but caught in a current of academic dips that have derailed others before. This isn't the end of your story—many rebound spectacularly by targeting schools where you shine brighter or bolstering those weak spots first. You've got the drive; now it's about reshaping that narrative for the next chapter. Keep pushing.
